coredata.dc:title: A dual-signal readout sensor for highly sensitive detection of iodide ions in urine based on catalase-like reaction of iodide ions and N-doped C-dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Haiyan
Lu, Qiujun
Liu, Yalan
Li, Huan
Zhang, Youyu
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection method for iodide ions (I−) was developed. In the presence of I−, the color of N-doped C-dots/H2O2/OPD system aqueous solution varied from colorless to yellow due to 2,3-diaminophenazine (OPDox) produced from I−-catalyzed H2O2-o-phenylenediamine (OPD) reaction. At the same time, the OPDox had an excellent fluorescence (FL) property. Under the excitation wavelength at 390nm, a novel FL emission ascribed to OPDox was clearly observed at 565nm. In addition, the FL at 565nm gradually increased, while the FL of emission peak at 450nm attributed to N-doped C-dots gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of I− due to the FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) between N-doped C-dots and OPDox. Therefore, a dual-signal optical sensor was developed for detection of I−. Under the optimized conditions, the linear response to I− was in the range of 0.09–50μM and the limit of detection was 0.06μM. Moreover, this proposed assay was successfully applied for the detection of I− in urine. These results indicate that the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence dual-readout assay method has great potential for applications in physiological and pathological diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped C-dots
Colorimetric detection
Ratiometric fluorescence detection
Iodide ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection procedure of I−
The stability of the N-doped C-dots
Colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence response of detection system toward I−
Selectivity for detection of I− in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation of the Science & Technology Department of Hunan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475043, 21275051), Doctoral Fund of Ministry of Education of China (NO: 20134306110006) and the Foundation of the Science & Technology Department of Hunan Province (2016SK2020).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of I−-sensing mechanism based on N-doped C-dots/H2O2/OPD.
(A) Typical TEM and HRTEM (inset) image of the as-prepared N-doped C-dots. (B) FT-IR spectra of N-doped C-dots.

(A) UV–vis spectra of N-doped C-dots and OPDox, respectively. Maximum excitation at 348nm and maximum emission at 444nm of N-doped C-dots, respectively. (B) FL emission spectra of N-doped C-dots at different excitation wavelengths that change from 300nm to 420nm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

FL spectra of N-doped C-dots solution (a), N-doped C-dots/H2O2 solution (b), N-doped C-dots/I− solution (c), N-doped C-dots/OPD solution (d), N-doped C-dots/H2O2/I− solution (e), N-doped C-dots/I−/OPD solution (f), N-doped C-dots/H2O2/OPD solution (g), H2O2/I−/OPD solution (h) and N-doped C-dots/H2O2/I−/OPD solution (i), respectively. [N-doped C-dots]=0.015mgmL−1, [OPD]=0.5mM, [H2O2]=5mM, [I−]=50μM. The buffer solution is NaAc-HAc (20mM, pH 5.0). The incubation temperature and incubation time were 39°C and 80min, respectively. The excitation wavelength is 390nm.

(A) The effect of concentration of N-doped C-dots to OPDox, a line represents UV–vis spectra of OPDox, b line represents UV–vis spectra of 0.025mgmL−1 N-doped C-dots, c line represents UV–vis spectra of 0.015mgmL−1 N-doped C-dots, d line represents UV–vis spectra of 0.005mgmL−1 N-doped C-dots. The effects of (B) different incubation temperature, (C) different pH value, (D) different H2O2 concentration, (E) different OPD concentration and (F) different incubation time for N-doped C-dots/H2O2/I−/OPD system. The optimal condition: [N-doped C-dots]=0.015mgmL−1, [H2O2]=5mM, [OPD]=0.5mM. The temperature, incubation time and pH are 39°C, 80min and 5.0, respectively. The buffer solution is NaAc-HAc (20mM, pH 5.0). The excitation wavelength is 390nm. F 565 refers to the emission intensity at 565nm of OPDox. F 450 refers to the emission intensity at 450nm of N-doped C-dots. F 565/F 450 is the ratio of the emission intensity at 565nm versus the emission intensity at 450nm.

(A and B) Relationship between the absorbance (440nm) intensity of OPDox and different concentration of I− (0, 0.09, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50μM). (C) FL responses of N-doped C-dots/H2O2/OPD system in the presence of different concentration of I−. (D) The relationship between the F 565/F 450 and different concentration of I− (0, 0.09, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50μM). [N-doped C-dots]=0.015mgmL−1, [OPD]=0.5mM, [H2O2]=5mM. The buffer solution is NaAc-HAc (20mM, pH 5.0). The incubation temperature and incubation time were 39°C and 80min, respectively. The excitation wavelength is 390nm. F 565 refers to the emission intensity at 565nm of OPDox. F 450 refers to the emission intensity at 450nm of N-doped C-dots. F 565/F 450 is the ratio of the emission intensity at 565nm versus the emission intensity at 450nm.

FL intensity ratio (F 565/F 450) of N-doped C-dots/H2O2/OPD in the presence of anions. (a) blank, (b) K+, (c) Na+, (d) CO3 2−, (e) NO3−, (f) SO3 2−, (g) SO4 2−, (h) HPO4 2−, (i) Ac−, (j) SCN−, (k) F−, (l) Cl−, (m) Br−, (n) I−. [N-doped C-dots]=0.015mgmL−1, [OPD]=0.5mM, [H2O2]=5mM, [I−]=0.5μM, [Br−]=20μM. The concentration of the other anions is 50μM. The buffer solution is NaAc-HAc (20mM, pH 5.0). The excitation wavelength is 390nm. F 565 refers to the emission intensity at 565nm of OPDox. F 450 refers to the emission intensity at 450nm of N-doped C-dots. F 565/F 450 is the ratio of the emission intensity at 565nm versus the emission intensity at 450nm.
Determination of I− in urine samples using the proposed method with dual-signal.

head.ce:title: A dual-signal readout sensor for highly sensitive detection of iodide ions in urine based on catalase-like reaction of iodide ions and N-doped C-dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection method for iodide ions (I−) was developed. In the presence of I−, the color of N-doped C-dots/H2O2/OPD system aqueous solution varied from colorless to yellow due to 2,3-diaminophenazine (OPDox) produced from I−-catalyzed H2O2-o-phenylenediamine (OPD) reaction. At the same time, the OPDox had an excellent fluorescence (FL) property. Under the excitation wavelength at 390nm, a novel FL emission ascribed to OPDox was clearly observed at 565nm. In addition, the FL at 565nm gradually increased, while the FL of emission peak at 450nm attributed to N-doped C-dots gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of I− due to the FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) between N-doped C-dots and OPDox. Therefore, a dual-signal optical sensor was developed for detection of I−. Under the optimized conditions, the linear response to I− was in the range of 0.09–50μM and the limit of detection was 0.06μM. Moreover, this proposed assay was successfully applied for the detection of I− in urine. These results indicate that the colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence dual-readout assay method has great potential for applications in physiological and pathological diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Iodide ion (I−) is an indispensable microelement to humans and is essential part of the thyroid hormones that play an important role in mental development, metabolism, neurological activity, cell growth and thyroid function [1,2]. The World Health Organization establishes the consumption of iodine as 150μg or 250μg per day in adults and lactating or pregnant [3], respectively. Deficiency of I− can result in some disorders, such as goiter, hypothyroidism, increased infant mortality, spontaneous abortion and mental retardation, whereas the excess may cause disorders in the thyroid functioning and autoimmune diseases [4,5]. Therefore, a fast and reliable analytical method for the detection of trace levels of I− is essential to avoid excessive intake or deficiency. In order to detect I− levels, several methods such as surface-enhanced Raman scattering [6], ion chromatography [7], liquid chromatography [8], coupled plasma mass spectrometry [9] and electrochemical detection [10–12] have been developed. Although these procedures allow achieving reliable results for determination of I−, multi-step sample preparation, the complexity of instrumentation, specialized skills, and time-consuming processes affected the application of these methods. Compared to these methods, optical-based analytical methods have attracted growing interest in biological analytical work due to high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, short response time and directly visualization of response. Nowadays, some small molecules based-fluorescent sensors were designed by organic synthesis to detect I− [13–15]. In spite of high selectivity for I−, these methods suffered from some drawbacks, such as tedious chemical modification, complicated pure process, toxic and side effect, non-water solubility and expensive cost. Thus, efforts were devoted to designing simple, low cost and environmentally-friendly sensor for the determination of I− in biological samples.

In recent years, the application of nanomaterials in the development of sensor has attracted great interests due to their unique optical properties [16–19]. Among these nanomaterials, carbon nanodots (C-dots) have been widely investigated [20,21]. The advantages of C-dots include high fluorescence (FL) intensity, good chemical stability, low damage to analytes, photostability, cheap to produce and easy to handle, promising for optical-based analytical applications. For example, C-dots as FL sensors for detection of I− were reported through the FL recovery of the quenched C-dots–Hg2+ due to the stronger binding force between I− and Hg2+ and the formation of HgI2 [22,23]. C-dots as effective fluorescent probe were developed by Zhang et al. for sensitive and selective detection of I− based on the strong electrostatic interaction between C-dots and I− [24]. Gao et al. reported a label-free and turn on FL sensor for selective detection of I− based on the principle of FL recovery of C-dots through the strong and specific interaction between I− and silver nanoparticles on the surface of C-dots/silver nanocomposites [25]. Although these sensors possessed simple instruments, easy operations and high sensitivity and selectivity for I−, these reported FL sensors for I− were based on FL quenching or enhancement. That means the only detection signal is the FL intensity at a single wavelength, leading to interfere with the signal output due to detection surroundings, instrumental efficiency and environmental conditions. By contrast, ratiometric fluorescence sensors enable the measurement of emission intensities at two different wavelengths, thus overcoming the drawbacks of the intensity based sensors. At the same time, sensing assays with dual signals can increase the dynamic range of FL measurements and provide a built-in correction for environmental and concentration factors. However, to the best our knowledge, few multi-signal detection systems for I− has been reported in literature. With consideration of the above argument, new materials with excellent properties and suitable designs should be developed to gain sensor owning superior performance.

Herein, a novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detection of I−was developed. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped C-dots) with high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of 29.9% were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal approach using ammonium citrate and o-aminobenzoic acid as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. I−-sensing mechanism based on catalase-like reaction of I− and N-doped C-dots was shown in Scheme 1 . Experimental results indicated that the sensor could be applied to discriminate I− from several normal cations and anions. This assay method was successfully applied for the detection of I− in urine with satisfied result.

Ammonium citrate, o-aminobenzoic acid, hydrogen peroxide, sulfate iron, quinoline sulfate, KCl, NaCl, NaF, Na2CO3, NaNO3, Na2SO4, HPO4 2−, NaAC, KSCN, KBr, KI and o-phenylenediamine were obtained from Shanghai Reagent (Shanghai, China). The human urine was obtained from Hospital of Hunan Normal University. The standard KI solution was prepared using Millipore Milli-Q ultrapur water, and stocked at 4°C. FLQY was determined using a previously published procedure by using quinine sulfate as a reference standard (FLQY=54%). All other chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade. Millipore Milli-Q ultrapur water (Millipore, ≥18MΩcm−1, U.S.A) was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were collected from a JEOL-1230 transmission electronic microscope (JEOL, Japan). The UV–vis (UV–vis) spectra were obtained by a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimazu Co., Japan). An F-7000 FL spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Japan) was applied for the FL analysis. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet Nexus 670 FT-IR spectroscope (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA). The ζ potential measurement of N-doped C-dots and OPDox were performed on the Nano-ZS Zetasizer ZEN3600 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK).

The N-doped C-dots were prepared by hydrothermal method using ammonium citrate and o-aminobenzoic acid as precursors. Briefly, 1.0g ammonium citrate and 0.5g o-aminobenzoic acid were firstly dissolved into 10mL water under the constantly stirring. Then the solution was transferred into a 25mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. Afterward, the sealed autoclave was heated to 180°C in an electric oven and kept for 4h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 10000rpm for 5min to remove the large size particles and dialyzed against water through a dialysis bag (cut-off molecular weight 1000Da) for 1day. The suspension of C-dots was finally obtained and stored at 4°C for further characterization and applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection procedure of I−
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 1mL KI solutions with different concentrations were prepared by diluting of different volume of standard KI solution using NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.02M, pH 5.0). 10μL of KI with different concentrations was mixed with 100μL C-dots, 50μL of 0.1M H2O2, 10μL of 5×10−2 M OPD and 830μL NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.02M, pH=5.0). Next, the mixed solution was incubated in a 39°C water bath for 80min and then cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, color photos were taken immediately. The N-doped C-dots/H2O2/OPD/I− system had obvious absorbance at 440nm, which was recorded at 440nm Abs. At the same time, FL emission spectra of the system were measured under the excitation wavelength at 390nm.

The urine was obtained from Hospital of Hunan Normal University. The urine was adjusted to pH 5.0 with NaAc-HAc buffer solution to obtain desired urine concentration (10%). Then, the standard addition method was used to test the applicability of the sensor.

To study the morphology of N-doped C-dots, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was characterized. These results are shown in Fig. 1 A. TEM images show that N-doped C-dots are of low size dispersion. The size distributions of N-doped C-dots are shown in Fig. S1, indicating the diameters range from 1.5nm to 3.0nm. The HRTEM image of the C-dots shows a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21nm (inset in Fig. 1A), which attributes to the (100) diffraction planes of graphitic carbon [26,27]. The functional groups on surface of N-doped C-dots were then investigated using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. As shown in Fig. 1B, the FT-IR spectra of N-doped C-dots, exhibits distinct absorption bands at 3225cm−1, which can be attributed to stretching vibrations of OH [28]. The weak band at 1734cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CO [29]. The characteristic peak at 1521cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CC, CN and NO [30]. Additionally, the peak located at 1342cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of CC, CO and CN [31]. These above results confirmed that nitrogen element was successfully doped at the C-dots and the as-synthesized nanoparticles were functionalized by hydroxyl and carboxyl.

In addition, the optical properties of N-doped C-dots were investigated by the UV–vis absorption and the FL spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 A, N-doped C-dots aqueous solution has a broad absorption spectrum at 330nm, which attribute to the n-π* transition of the CO bond [32,33]. The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of the N-doped C-dots aqueous solution are 348nm and 444nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2B, the C-dots solution exhibits excitation-wavelength-dependent FL properties with emission peaks ranging from 390nm to 455nm at excitation from 300nm to 420nm. This behavior may reflect not only effects from particles of different sizes in the sample but also a distribution of different emissive trap sites on each N-doped C-dots [34–37]. The different “surface states” by hydroxyl amino and carboxyl functional groups, which was exhibited by FT-IR analysis in Fig. 1B, determined different energy levels. Therefore, under difficult excitation wavelength, the emissive trap sites on differently sized N-doped C-dots surface emit FL, and the emission spectrum occurred shift because of optical selection of differently sized N-doped C-dots and different FL emissive intensity on N-doped C-dots surface [34–37]. The FLQY of N-doped C-dots was measured to be about 29.9% with quinine sulfate in 0.1M sulfuric acid solution (FLQY 54%) as reference [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of the N-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the stability of N-doped C-dots, FL intensity of the N-doped C-dots solution under extreme pH, high NaCl concentration or long time illumination was measured. Large variations of FL intensity of the N-doped C-dots did not take place when pH values were varied from 2.0 to 12.0 (Fig. S2A). It was deduced that the N-doped C-dots solution owned well properties of acid resistance and alkaline resistance. In addition, FL intensity of the prepared C-dots did not obviously change with the concentration of NaCl was kept 2.5M (Fig. S2B). The result indicated that the N-doped C-dots owned an outstanding property of salt stability. What was more, it was found that the N-doped C-dots still exhibited good photo-stability when N-doped C-dots stored at room temperature at a month later (Fig. S2C). It was observed that there was not obvious alteration of FL intensity of the C-dots solution during continuous irradiation by a Xe lamp at 360nm (Fig. S2D). The result indicated the C-dots exhibited excellent resistance to photo-bleaching [39].

The working principle of this sensing system with dual signal was illustrated in Scheme 1. In the presence of I−, the catalysis of I− upon the reaction of H2O2 with OPD was occurred, and OPD was oxidized to 2, 3-diaminophenazine (OPDox), resulting in a color change from colorless to yellow under naked eye observation [40,41]. As shown at blue line in Fig. S3, a new absorption peak which attributed to the absorption peak of OPDox, appeared at 440nm. At the same time, the OPDox had an excellent FL property. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the FL emission at 565nm ascribed to OPDox (curve h). FL emission at 450nm of N-doped C-dots have no obvious change in the presence of H2O2 (curve b), I− (curve c), OPD (curve d), H2O2/I− (curve e), I−/OPD (curve f), H2O2/OPD solution (curve g), indicating H2O2, I−, OPD, H2O2/I−, I−/OPD, H2O2/OPD exhibit little effect for N-doped C-dots. However, the FL intensity at 450nm of N-doped C-dots decreased in the presence of H2O2/I−/OPD (curve i). The FL emission spectrum of N-doped C-dots overlapped with the absorption spectrum of OPDox (Fig. 2A). The possible FL quenching mechanisms of N-doped C-dots could be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [42]. To investigate the mechanism, UV–vis absorption spectra of N-doped C-dots, OPDox and N-doped C-dots/OPDox were studied, respectively (Fig. S3). The absorption peak at 418nm of OPDox occurred red shift in the presence of N-doped C-dots, indicating the inter-particle distance was short and a new complex was formed. Therefore, FRET between N-doped C-dots and OPDox could occur. To further confirm the mechanism, the Nano-ZS Zetasizer ZEN 3600 was used to measure the zeta potential of N-doped C-dots and OPDox in NaAc-HAc buffer solution (Fig. S4). The zeta potential of N-doped C-dots and OPDox are −31.0mV and +21.5mV, respectively, indicating that OPDox could be adsorbed on the surface of N-doped C-dots through electrostatic interaction. The distance between the N-doped C-dots and OPDox should be less than 10nm. Therefore, the FRET between N-doped C-dots and OPDox could occur [43]. Thus, the FL of N-doped C-dots was efficiently quenched by FRET. The FL quenching effect of OPDox on N-doped C-dots was investigated by Stern–Volmer analysis [44]. The Stern–Volmer plot of FL quenching was shown in Fig. S5, there is a good linearity between the I 0/I and the concentrations of I− in the range of 0–7μM. The quenching constant (Ksv) was calculated according to the Stern-Volmer:
I 0/I =Ksv ×[Q]+1
Where I 0 and I are the FL intensity of N-doped C-dots in the absence and presence of OPDox, respectively, and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher, OPDox. And the Stern-Volmer equations fitted as:
I 0/ I =0.125×[Q]+1
The Ksv was estimated to be 1.25×10−7 M−1, which suggests that OPDox could efficiently quench FL of N-doped C-dots. Therefore, FL of N-doped C-dots should be quenched by OPDox through FRET [45–47].

In order to obtain better detection sensitivity, it is necessary to optimize experimental conditions such as the concentration of N-doped C-dots, incubation temperature, solution pH value, the concentration of H2O2 and OPD, and incubation time of catalytic reaction. The concentration of N-doped C-dots is a key factor affecting the sensitivity of the proposed method. The reason can be attributed to that insufficient N-doped C-dots can affect the sensitivity of the proposed sensor, while excess N-doped C-dots can disturb the colorimetric signal at 440nm of OPDox. As shown in Fig. 4 A, when the concentration of N-doped C-dots was 0.015mgmL−1, Abs. of N-doped C-dots at 440nm was 0, avoiding to disturb the colorimetric detection at 440nm of OPDox. Furthermore, the ratio of the emission intensity at 565nm of OPDox versus the emission intensity at 450nm of N-doped C-dot (0.015mgmL−1) was almost close to 1:1, which could improve the sensitivity of colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection, on the basis of the report existing in the literature [48]. Therefore, 0.015mgmL−1 N-doped C-dots was used in the experiment. The temperature is also a key factor that affects the proposed method, because catalytic reaction is incubation temperature-dependent. As shown in Fig. 4B, the highest value of F 565/F 450 was obtained at 39°C. As too low incubation temperature could affect sensing sensitivity of the proposed method, while too high incubation temperature could cause H2O2 decomposition. Thus, to achieve better detection sensitivity, the optimum temperature of catalytic reaction for the I− determination is at 39°C. The effect of pH on the F 565/F 450 of detecting system was studied in the range of 2.0–7.0. These results indicated that the highest value of F 565/F 450 was obtained at pH 5.0 (Fig. 4C), so pH 5.0 was regarded as the optimal pH value. The effect of concentration of H2O2 was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4D, the result indicated that the F 565/F 450 reached a maximum value at 5mM H2O2, so the 5mM H2O2 was used in this experiment. Fig. 4E shown the effect of concentration of OPD on the F 565/F 450 of detecting system, the maximum F 565/F 450 was observed when 0.5mM OPD was added into catalytic reaction system. Therefore, 0.5mM OPD was used in the following experiments. The effect of incubation time of catalytic reaction was also investigated (Fig. 4F). The result indicated that F 565/F 450 reached maximum and become stabled when the incubation time was 80min. Too long incubation time could affect the sensitivity of the proposed method due to the poor stability I−. Thus, the optimal incubation time of 80min was used in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence response of detection system toward I−
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions discussed above, the linear response range of sensing system was measured. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the absorbance peak centered at 440nm increase gradually with the increasing concentration of I−. As shown in Fig. 5B, the calibration curve for I− has good linearity for concentrations ranging from 0.09 to 10μM and 10–50μM with the following equation: y =−0.00914+0.0557 x and y =0.439+0.00865x, respectively, where y refers to the measured absorbance and x refers to the concentration of I−. These correlation coefficients are 0.994 and 0.993, respectively. The detection limit is as low as 0.06μM (S/N=3). Inset in Fig. 5B is the digital picture of N-doped C-dots/H2O2/OPD in the presence of I− with different concentrations, suggesting that the color changes induced by I−. As shown in Fig. 5C, the result indicated that FL signal responses were concentration-dependent. Upon excitation at 390nm, the emission intensity at 565nm of the OPDox gradually enhances, while the emission at 450nm of N-doped C-dots gradually decreases with the increasing concentration of I−. As demonstrated in Fig. 5D, the F 565/F 450 increases linearly with the concentration of I− in the range of 0.09–50μM. The following equation is y =0.143+0.111 x (R 2 =0.991), where y and x refer to the measured F 565/F 450 and the concentration of I−, respectively. The detection limit is as low as 0.06μM (S/N=3), which is lower than other reports (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity for detection of I− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Besides sensitivity, selectivity is another important parameter to evaluate the performance of the sensing system. Therefore, under the same condition, the selectivity of this approach was examined by using the assay for the sensing of several common cations and anions including Na+, K+, CO3 2−, NO3−, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, HPO4 2−, Ac−, SCN−, F−, Cl−, Br− and I−. And none of the other cations and anions, except I−, induced an apparent colour change from colourless to yellow and an obviously F 565/F 450 change (Fig. 6 ). It is known that halide ions contain F−, Cl−, Br− and I−, which exhibit similar property such as catalytic oxidation (F− <Cl− <Br− <I−) [49]. Br− exhibited some interference. However, the normal reference concentration of Br− is much less than of I− in urine [50,51]. Therefore, the potential interference from Br− and the other ions can be ignored in urine assays. The excellent selectivity and specificity could be probably attributed to I−-catalyzed H2O2-OPD reaction and FRET between N-doped C-dots and OPDox.

The feasibility of the proposed sensor based on N-doped C-dots for detecting I− in real samples was discussed by urine (10%). As shown in Table 1 , we obtained satisfactory result. The recovery of the spiked samples ranged from 98.0% to 102.0%. These results suggested that our strategy was likely to be capable of practically useful I− detection in biological samples.

In summary, a novel and sensitive colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence detection method for detection of I− based on catalase-like reaction of I−and N-doped C-dots was developed. The dual-readout assay method has many advantages, such as multi-signal response, low environmental interference, low detection limit and high selectivity. The proposed method could selectively detect I− from several normal cations and anions and could be applied to detect I− in human urine. Our study may give a new sight for advanced application of N-doped C-dots in sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475043, 21275051), Doctoral Fund of Ministry of Education of China (NO: 20134306110006) and the Foundation of the Science & Technology Department of Hunan Province (2016SK2020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.04.117.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colorimetric sensing of iodide based on triazole-acetamide functionalized gold nanoparticles
A colorimetric method for highly sensitive and accurate detection of iodide by finding the critical color in a color change process using silver triangular nanoplates
A portable multi-syringe flow system for spectrofluorimetric determination of iodide in seawater
Pervaporation-flow injection with chemiluminescence detection for determination of iodide in multivitamin tablets
Reappraisal of risk of iodine-induced hyperthyroidism: an epidemiological population survey
Highly sensitive and selective determination of iodide and thiocyanate concentrations using surface-enhanced Raman scattering of starch-reduced gold nanoparticles
Iodide analysis by ion chromatography on a new stationary phase of polystyrene-divinylbenzene agglomerated with polymerized-epichlorohydrin-dimethylamine
Determination of iodide in seawater using C30 column modified with polyoxyethylene oleyl ether in ion chromatography
Iodine determination in biological materials by ICP-MS
Electrochemical quantification of iodide ions in synthetic urine using silver nanoparticles: a proof-of-concept
Preparation of iodide selective carbon paste electrode with modified carbon nanotubes by potentiometric method and effect of CuS-NPs on its response
Screen-printed electrodes for amperometric determination of iodide
A highly sensitive and selective turn-on fluorescence sensor for iodide detection based on newly synthesized oligopyrrole derivative and application to real samples
Carbazole and triazole-containing conjugated polymer as a visual and fluorometric probe for iodide and mercury
A simple coumarin-based colorimetric and ratiometric chemosensor for acetate and a selective fluorescence turn-on probe for iodide
Mixed ligand system of cysteine and thioglycolic acid assisting in the synthesis of highly luminescent water-soluble CdTe nanorods
Aptamer-modified graphene-based catalytic micromotors: off–on fluorescent detection of ricin
Direct electrochemistry and electrocatalysis based on film of horseradish peroxidase intercalated into layered titanate nano-sheets
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Carbon quantum dot-functionalized aerogels for NO2 gas sensing
Fluorescence determination of nitrite in water using prawn-shell derived nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots as fluorophores
Highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as effective fluorescent probes for mercuric and iodide ions
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iodide
Determination of iodide via direct fluorescence quenching at nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot fluorophores
A label-free and turn-on fluorescence sensor for sensitive and selective detection of iodide using carbon nanodots/silver nanocomposites
Extraction of electrochemiluminescent oxidized carbon quantum dots from activated carbon
Fast one-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots bypyrolyzing ethanolamine
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur-and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Non-enzymatic-browning-reaction: a versatile route for production of nitrogen-doped carbon dots with tunable multicolor luminescent display
Fast one-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots by pyrolyzing ethanolamine
Economical, Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) Ions
Control the size and surface chemistry of graphene for the rising fluorescent materials
Nitrogen-doped carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate
Synthesis and drug detection performance of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application
Nitrogen-doped carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate
Graphitic carbon nitride nanodots: as reductant for the synthesis of silver nanoparticles and its biothiols biosensing application
Magnetic colorimetric immunoassay for human interleukin-6 based on the oxidase activity of ceria spheres
Nanosensor composed of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for highly selective detection of cysteine with multiple signals
Nanosecond fluorescence resonance energy transfer-fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy to localize the protein interactions in a single living cell
Electrospun nanofibrous membranes for highly sensitive optical sensors
Glutathione regulation-based dual-functional upconversion sensing-platform for acetylcholinesterase activity and cadmium ions
An upconversion fluorescence resonance energy transfer nanosensor for one step detection of melamine in raw milk
A carbon dot based biosensor for melamine detection by fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Dual-emissive nanohybrid of carbon dots and gold nanoclusters for sensitive determination of mercuric ions
Core/shell Cu@Ag nanoparticle: a versatile platform for colorimetric visualization of inorganic anions
Bromine levels in human serum urine, hair
Smaller thyroid gland volume with high urinary iodine excretion in Japanese schoolchildren: normative reference values in an iodine-sufficient area and comparison with the WHO/ICCIDD reference

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Haiyan Wang is a doctoral student in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, China.

Qiujun Lu is a doctoral student in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, China.

Yalan Liu is a graduate student in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, China.

Huan Li is a graduate student in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, China.

Youyu Zhang is a Professor in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University. She received her MSc degree from Hunan Normal University and Doctor degree from State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biological Sensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, China. Her research is focused on chemical and biological sensing. Her current research interests are in the areas nanomaterials and nanoanalysis.

Shuozhuo Yao is a Professor in Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University and he is an academician of Chinese Academy of Science as well.
